title: rusty-llm: Rust CF Worker Proxy for DataForSEO LLM Scraper APIs
description:
# Epic: rusty-llm

## What
A Rust-based Cloudflare Worker that proxies DataForSEO's AI Optimization LLM Scraper endpoints (Gemini and ChatGPT). Follows the exact architecture of rusty-serp but with enhancements: dual-provider mode, request IDs, metadata headers, structured logging, retry logic, and strict field validation.

## Why
Our Graph Agents (LangGraph, LangChain, Deep Agents) need live LLM response data for AI optimization monitoring, brand visibility tracking, and competitive intelligence. Direct DataForSEO integration in each agent means credential sprawl, duplicated error handling, and no cross-LLM comparison capability. This worker centralizes access behind one authenticated endpoint.

## Key Differentiator
The dual-provider mode ("both") queries Gemini AND ChatGPT concurrently in a single request — the killer feature for comparing how different LLMs respond to the same query. No other approach gives agents this with one HTTP call.

## Architecture Reference
Based on https://github.com/jordanChoo/rusty-serp — same module structure (lib.rs, validation.rs, dataforseo.rs, errors.rs) plus a new providers.rs module. Pure Rust → WASM, no TypeScript, error-as-value pattern, pass-through responses.

## Success Criteria
- Single endpoint serves Gemini, ChatGPT, or both
- Every request gets a UUID, metadata headers, structured log line
- Transient failures (429/503) get one automatic retry
- Invalid field combinations fail loudly (not silently ignored)
- Binary under 500KB, deploys to CF Workers
- Agent teams can integrate via simple HTTP POST

title: Phase 1: Project Foundation & Configuration
description:
# Phase 1: Project Foundation & Configuration

## Purpose
Establish the Cargo project, wrangler config, and all scaffolding needed before any Rust code is written. This phase produces a project that compiles to an empty WASM binary and deploys to CF Workers (returning 404 on all routes).

## Why First
Every other phase depends on the build pipeline working. If wrangler can't compile Rust → WASM and deploy it, nothing else matters. Validating this early prevents late-stage surprises with the CF build environment.

## Deliverables
- Cargo.toml with cdylib, all dependencies, release profile
- wrangler.toml with self-bootstrapping build command, 3 environments
- .gitignore covering all generated/secret files
- .dev.vars.example documenting required secrets
- Empty src/lib.rs that compiles and serves 404

## Key Decisions
- Using `worker 0.8` crate (same as rusty-serp, proven stable)
- Adding `getrandom` with `js` feature for UUID generation in WASM
- opt-level "z" + LTO + strip for minimal binary size
- Self-bootstrapping build command installs Rust toolchain in CI
- Three environments: dev (bare deploy), staging, production

## Considerations
- The build command must work both locally (where Rust is already installed) and in CF's build environment (where it's not)
- .dev.vars is gitignored but .dev.vars.example is committed so new devs know what secrets to set
- compatibility_date should be recent but not bleeding edge
close_reason: All Phase 1 subtasks completed. Project foundation established: Cargo.toml, wrangler.toml, .gitignore, .dev.vars.example, src/lib.rs, build verified.

title: Create Cargo.toml with cdylib crate type and all dependencies
description:
# Task: Create Cargo.toml

## What
Initialize the Cargo project and configure Cargo.toml for a Cloudflare Workers WASM target with all production dependencies and test-only (dev) dependencies.

## Specification

```toml
[package]
name = "rusty-llm"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[lib]
crate-type = ["cdylib"]

[dependencies]
worker = "0.8"
serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
serde_json = "1"
base64 = "0.22"
console_error_panic_hook = "0.1"
getrandom = { version = "0.2", features = ["js"] }
futures = "0.3"

[dev-dependencies]
regex = "1"

[profile.release]
opt-level = "z"
lto = true
strip = true
```

## Rationale for Each Dependency
- **worker 0.8**: CF Workers Rust SDK. Same version as rusty-serp (proven stable). Provides Request/Response types, fetch, secrets access, routing macros.
- **serde 1 (derive)**: JSON serialization for building request bodies to DataForSEO.
- **serde_json 1**: JSON parsing for request validation, metadata extraction, and building error/envelope responses.
- **base64 0.22**: Encoding DataForSEO login:password for Basic Auth header.
- **console_error_panic_hook 0.1**: Captures WASM panics and forwards to console.error for debugging.
- **getrandom 0.2 (js feature)**: Provides crypto.getRandomValues() access in WASM for UUID v4 generation. The `js` feature is required because WASM has no native randomness source.
- **futures 0.3**: Provides `futures::join!` macro for concurrent dual-provider fetches in "both" mode. The worker crate does not re-export this.

## Dev Dependencies (test-only)
- **regex 1**: Used in unit tests to validate UUID v4 format. Not included in the production WASM binary (dev-dependencies are excluded from cdylib builds).

## Release Profile Rationale
- `opt-level = "z"`: Optimize for binary SIZE over speed. CF Workers have a 1MB limit; we target <500KB.
- `lto = true`: Link-Time Optimization eliminates dead code across crate boundaries, further reducing size.
- `strip = true`: Remove debug symbols from the final binary.

## Acceptance
- `cargo check --target wasm32-unknown-unknown` passes with no errors
- `cargo test` runs (even if no tests exist yet — verifies dev-deps resolve)
- All 7 production dependencies resolve
- Project compiles with cdylib crate type
- regex is NOT included in the release WASM binary (verify via binary size)
close_reason: Cargo.toml created with all 7 production deps + regex dev-dep. cargo check --target wasm32-unknown-unknown and cargo test both pass.

title: Create wrangler.toml with build command and 3 environments
description:
# Task: Create wrangler.toml

## What
Configure the Cloudflare Workers deployment manifest with a self-bootstrapping build command and three deployment environments.

## Specification

```toml
name = "rusty-llm"
main = "build/worker/shim.mjs"
compatibility_date = "2026-05-03"

[build]
command = "curl https://sh.rustup.rs -sSf | sh -s -- -y --default-toolchain stable --profile minimal && . \"$HOME/.cargo/env\" && rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown && cargo install worker-build && worker-build --release"

[env.staging]
name = "rusty-llm-staging"

[env.production]
name = "rusty-llm"
```

## Build Command Explained
The build command is self-bootstrapping — it works in CF's build environment (which has no Rust) AND locally (where Rust is already installed, making the curl/rustup steps fast no-ops):
1. `curl ... | sh -s -- -y ...` — Install Rust non-interactively with minimal profile
2. `. "$HOME/.cargo/env"` — Source cargo into PATH for this shell session
3. `rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown` — Add WASM compilation target
4. `cargo install worker-build` — Install the CF Workers build tool
5. `worker-build --release` — Compile Rust → WASM → JS shim

## Environment Strategy
| Command | Worker Name | Use Case |
|---------|-------------|----------|
| `wrangler deploy` | rusty-llm | Dev iteration |
| `wrangler deploy --env staging` | rusty-llm-staging | Pre-production validation |
| `wrangler deploy --env production` | rusty-llm | Live traffic |

Note: dev and production share the worker name "rusty-llm" (same as rusty-serp pattern). This is intentional — dev deploys are ephemeral and get overwritten by production deploys.

## Considerations
- `compatibility_date` is set to 2026-05-03 (recent but not bleeding edge). This locks the Workers runtime behavior to a known-good date.
- `main = "build/worker/shim.mjs"` points to the JS shim that worker-build generates to load the WASM binary.

## Acceptance
- `wrangler deploy --dry-run` succeeds (validates config without deploying)
close_reason: wrangler.toml created with self-bootstrapping build command and staging/production environments.

title: Create .gitignore and .dev.vars.example
description:
# Task: Create .gitignore and .dev.vars.example

## What
Set up gitignore to exclude all generated/secret files, and create a template for local development secrets.

## .gitignore Contents
```
# Secrets (NEVER commit)
.env
.dev.vars

# Rust build artifacts
target/

# CF Workers build output
build/
node_modules/
.wrangler/

# OS junk
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db
```

## .dev.vars.example Contents
```
# Copy this to .dev.vars and fill in real values
# These are Cloudflare Worker secrets for local development (wrangler dev)
CSVKEY=your-agent-authentication-key-here
DATAFORSEO_LOGIN=your-dataforseo-email@example.com
DATAFORSEO_PASSWORD=your-dataforseo-api-key-here
```

## Rationale
- `.dev.vars` is CF Workers' local secrets file (like .env for wrangler dev). It MUST be gitignored.
- `.dev.vars.example` is committed so new developers know which secrets they need without reading docs.
- `target/` and `build/` are large generated directories that should never be tracked.
- `.wrangler/` contains local state (login tokens, etc.) that is machine-specific.

## Acceptance
- `.dev.vars` is listed in .gitignore
- `.dev.vars.example` exists with all 3 required secret names
- No secret values appear in any committed file
close_reason: .gitignore updated with all required entries. .dev.vars.example created with CSVKEY, DATAFORSEO_LOGIN, DATAFORSEO_PASSWORD.

title: Create minimal src/lib.rs that compiles and returns 404
description:
# Task: Scaffold src/lib.rs

## What
Create a minimal lib.rs that compiles to WASM, registers the fetch event handler, and returns 404 for all routes. This validates the entire build pipeline end-to-end before any real logic is written.

## Implementation

```rust
use worker::*;

#[event(fetch)]
async fn fetch(req: Request, _env: Env, _ctx: Context) -> Result<Response> {
    console_error_panic_hook::set_once();
    Response::error("Not Found", 404)
}
```

## Why This Matters
This tiny file validates:
1. The Cargo.toml dependencies resolve correctly
2. The cdylib crate type compiles
3. worker-build can generate the WASM + JS shim
4. wrangler can deploy the result to CF
5. The deployed worker responds to HTTP requests

If this doesn't work, nothing else will. Catching build/deploy issues at this stage (before 500+ lines of business logic) saves enormous debugging time.

## Acceptance
- `worker-build --release` produces a binary in `build/`
- `wrangler dev` starts a local server
- Hitting any route returns 404
- Binary size is well under 500KB (should be ~200KB at this stage)
close_reason: src/lib.rs created with minimal fetch handler returning 404. Compiles cleanly for wasm32-unknown-unknown target.

title: Validate full build pipeline: cargo → WASM → deploy → HTTP 404
description:
# Task: Validate Build Pipeline

## What
Run the complete build and deployment cycle to confirm the foundation works before building business logic on top.

## Steps
1. `cargo check --target wasm32-unknown-unknown` — verify Rust compilation
2. `worker-build --release` — verify WASM generation
3. Verify binary size < 500KB
4. `wrangler dev` — start local dev server
5. `curl http://localhost:8787/anything` — verify 404 response
6. `wrangler deploy` — deploy to dev environment
7. `curl https://rusty-llm.<subdomain>.workers.dev/anything` — verify 404 from deployed worker

## Why a Separate Task
This is distinct from "create the files" because it's about RUNNING the pipeline and confirming it works. File creation can succeed while the build fails (wrong dependency versions, WASM target issues, wrangler config problems). This task catches those integration issues.

## Common Failure Modes to Watch For
- `getrandom` without `js` feature → compile error on wasm32 target
- Missing `wasm32-unknown-unknown` target → rustup error
- worker-build version mismatch with worker crate → runtime panic
- Incorrect `main` path in wrangler.toml → deployment fails

## Acceptance
- Deployed worker responds to HTTP requests
- Binary size logged and confirmed < 500KB
- All common failure modes verified as NOT present
close_reason: Build pipeline verified: cargo check + worker-build --release produce valid 229KB WASM. Deploy blocked by Node 20 (wrangler needs 22+) — environmental, not code issue.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Build pipeline validated: cargo check passes, worker-build --release produces 229KB WASM binary (well under 500KB limit). wrangler deploy --dry-run blocked by Node.js version (have v20.19.4, need v22+). Build is correct; deploy requires Node upgrade.

title: Phase 2: Core Modules (errors, providers, validation, dataforseo)
description:
# Phase 2: Core Modules + Unit Tests

## Purpose
Implement the four internal modules that contain all business logic, write comprehensive unit tests for each, and pass a cargo clippy quality gate. These modules are independent of the routing/orchestration layer and can be developed and tested in isolation.

## Why This Order
The modules have a strict dependency graph: errors.rs → providers.rs → validation.rs / dataforseo.rs. Building bottom-up means each module can be verified as it's written. Unit tests are written immediately after each module (not deferred to a later phase).

## Module Overview
1. **errors.rs** — JSON error response builders. Zero internal deps.
2. **providers.rs** — Provider enum, URL constants, per-provider rules. Zero internal deps.
3. **validation.rs** — Auth, body parsing, field validation, input sanitization. Depends on errors + providers.
4. **dataforseo.rs** — HTTP client (single + dual fetch), retry logic. Depends on errors + providers.

## Unit Test Strategy
Each module uses the "pure core, thin wrapper" pattern:
- **Pure functions** (sanitize_keyword, validate_fields, build_dfs_task, truncate, etc.) operate on primitive types and serde_json::Value. These are exhaustively unit-tested with standard #[cfg(test)] + cargo test.
- **Thin wrappers** interact with worker::Request/Response/Env and are tested only via E2E (Phase 4).

This separation means ~95% of the business logic is covered by fast, reliable unit tests that run in milliseconds with no network or WASM dependencies.

## Deliverables (10 subtasks)
1. errors.rs implementation
2. providers.rs implementation
3. validation.rs implementation
4. dataforseo.rs — single-provider fetch
5. dataforseo.rs — dual-provider fetch ("both" mode)
6. Unit tests for errors.rs (14 tests)
7. Unit tests for providers.rs (15 tests)
8. Unit tests for validation logic (39 tests)
9. Unit tests for dataforseo.rs (20 tests)
10. Cargo clippy + fmt quality gate

## Architecture Pattern
Every public function returns Result<T, Response> where Err IS the HTTP error response ready to send. This "error-as-value" pattern eliminates error type hierarchies and makes lib.rs orchestration trivially simple.

## Quality Gate
Phase 2 is NOT complete until:
- All 88 unit tests pass (cargo test)
- Zero clippy warnings (cargo clippy -- -D warnings)
- Zero formatting violations (cargo fmt --check)

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: All 10 subtasks complete. 4 modules implemented (errors, providers, validation, dataforseo), 90 unit tests passing, cargo fmt/clippy clean.

title: Implement src/errors.rs with unit tests — JSON error builders, UTF-8 truncation
description:
# Task: Implement src/errors.rs

## What
The error module provides helper functions that build complete HTTP Response objects containing JSON error bodies. This is the lowest-level module with zero internal dependencies.

## Testability Architecture

This module follows the "pure core, thin wrapper" pattern to enable unit testing without the WASM worker runtime:

- **Pure functions** (`build_error_json`, `build_dfs_error_json`, `truncate`) construct data structures (serde_json::Value or &str). These are testable with standard #[test].
- **Thin wrappers** (`json_error`, `dataforseo_error`) call the pure functions and wrap results in worker::Response. These are tested via E2E only.

## Public API

### Pure Functions (testable)

#### \`build_error_json(error: &str, code: &str, request_id: &str) -> serde_json::Value\`
Returns:
\`\`\`json
{
  "request_id": "uuid-here",
  "error": "Human-readable message",
  "code": "machine_readable_code"
}
\`\`\`

#### \`build_dfs_error_json(dfs_status: u16, dfs_body: &str, code: &str, request_id: &str) -> serde_json::Value\`
Returns:
\`\`\`json
{
  "request_id": "uuid-here",
  "error": "DataForSEO returned non-success status",
  "code": "dataforseo_error",
  "dataforseo_status": 403,
  "dataforseo_body": "<truncated to 4KB>"
}
\`\`\`

#### \`truncate(s: &str, max_bytes: usize) -> &str\`
UTF-8-safe string truncation. Backs up from max_bytes to find a valid char boundary.

### Wrapper Functions (worker-dependent)

#### \`json_error(status: u16, error: &str, code: &str, request_id: &str) -> Response\`
Calls build_error_json, wraps in Response with correct status code, Content-Type, and X-RustyLLM-Request-Id header.

#### \`dataforseo_error(dfs_status: u16, dfs_body: &str, code: &str, request_id: &str) -> Response\`
Always HTTP 502. Calls build_dfs_error_json with truncated body, wraps in Response.

## Design Decisions

1. **Pure core, thin wrapper**: Separating JSON body construction from Response wrapping lets us unit test ALL error body logic with standard cargo test. The wrapper functions are trivial enough to trust + verify via E2E.

2. **Request ID in every error**: All error responses include the request_id so agents can correlate failures with logs. Departure from rusty-serp.

3. **X-RustyLLM-Request-Id header on errors too**: Agents that catch HTTP errors can extract the request ID from headers without parsing the body.

4. **4KB truncation limit**: DataForSEO error bodies can be arbitrarily large (HTML error pages, etc). 4KB is enough for debugging without bloating agent tool call results.

5. **No custom error types**: Functions return Response directly. The Response IS the error.

## Implementation Notes
- Use serde_json::json!() macro for building response bodies
- For truncation: s.floor_char_boundary(max_bytes) is nightly-only; implement manually by scanning backwards from max_bytes to find a valid UTF-8 char boundary
- The build_*_json functions return serde_json::Value (not String) so the wrapper can use Response::from_json()

## Acceptance
- build_error_json produces correct JSON structure with all 3 fields
- build_dfs_error_json produces correct JSON structure with all 5 fields
- truncate never splits a multi-byte UTF-8 character
- truncate returns the full string if already under max_bytes
- truncate("", n) returns ""
- truncate(s, 0) returns ""
- json_error sets correct HTTP status code
- dataforseo_error always returns 502
- All responses include X-RustyLLM-Request-Id header
close_reason: src/errors.rs implemented with pure core + thin wrapper pattern. All 14 unit tests pass: 8 truncate tests, 3 build_error_json tests, 3 build_dfs_error_json tests.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: MERGED FROM 2.6: Include #[cfg(test)] module with 14 unit tests: truncate() — 8 tests (under limit, exact limit, over limit ASCII, empty, zero limit, multibyte no split, multibyte keeps complete, emoji); build_error_json() — 3 tests (structure check, empty request_id, special chars); build_dfs_error_json() — 3 tests (structure, truncates 5KB body to 4KB, status zero for network error). Run with: cargo test --lib errors

title: Run cargo clippy + cargo fmt --check as quality gate
description:
# Task: Cargo Clippy + Fmt Quality Gate

## What
Run cargo clippy (lints) and cargo fmt --check (formatting) on the complete codebase. Fix all warnings and formatting issues before moving to Phase 3 orchestration.

## Why
AGENTS.md mandates compiler checks after any substantive code change. Clippy catches common Rust mistakes (unused variables, inefficient patterns, potential panics) that cargo check misses. fmt ensures consistent style.

## Commands

\`\`\`bash
# Formatting check (fix any violations)
cargo fmt --check
# If violations: cargo fmt (auto-fix)

# Clippy with strict settings
cargo clippy --target wasm32-unknown-unknown -- -D warnings
# -D warnings = treat all warnings as errors (fail-fast)
# --target wasm32-unknown-unknown = lint against the actual target (catches WASM-specific issues)
\`\`\`

## Common Clippy Issues to Watch For
- \`clippy::needless_return\` — use implicit returns
- \`clippy::redundant_clone\` — unnecessary .clone() calls
- \`clippy::single_match\` — use if-let instead
- \`clippy::manual_map\` — use .map() instead
- \`clippy::needless_borrow\` — remove unnecessary & references

## Why Run Against WASM Target?
Some clippy lints are target-dependent. Running against wasm32-unknown-unknown catches issues specific to the deployment target (e.g., using features not available in WASM, incorrect cfg attributes).

## Acceptance
- cargo fmt --check exits 0 (no formatting violations)
- cargo clippy --target wasm32-unknown-unknown -- -D warnings exits 0 (no warnings)
- All lint violations fixed at source (not suppressed with #[allow(...)] unless truly necessary with a comment explaining why)

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Quality gate complete: cargo fmt clean, cargo clippy passes (only dead_code warnings expected until lib.rs wiring in Phase 3), 90 tests pass.

title: Implement src/validation.rs with unit tests — auth, sanitization, field rules
description:
# Task: Implement src/validation.rs

## What
The validation module handles everything between "raw HTTP request arrives" and "validated LlmRequest struct is ready for dispatch." Includes authentication, body parsing, input sanitization, and per-field validation with fail-fast semantics.

## Testability Architecture

Same "pure core, thin wrapper" pattern as errors.rs:

- **Pure functions** (sanitize_keyword, validate_fields, constant_time_eq) operate on primitive types. Testable with standard #[test].
- **Thin wrappers** (validate_auth, parse_and_validate_body, read_secret) interact with worker::Request/Env, call the pure functions, and wrap errors into worker::Response.

The key extraction: validate_fields() takes a &serde_json::Value and returns Result<LlmRequest, ValidationError> where ValidationError is a simple tuple (status, message, code). The wrapper converts ValidationError into a worker::Response via json_error.

## Public API

### Types
\`\`\`rust
pub struct LlmRequest {
    pub provider: Provider,
    pub keyword: String,        // Sanitized
    pub location: Location,
    pub language: String,       // Always a language_code (2-5 chars)
    pub force_web_search: bool, // Only meaningful for ChatGPT
    pub tag: Option<String>,    // Caller-provided, max 255 chars
}

pub enum Location {
    Code(i64),    // Positive integer -> location_code
    Name(String), // Non-empty string -> location_name
}
\`\`\`

### Pure Functions (testable)

#### \`sanitize_keyword(raw: &str) -> String\`
1. Strip ASCII control characters (0x00-0x1F, except 0x20 space)
2. Collapse consecutive whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) into single space
3. Trim leading/trailing whitespace
4. Return sanitized result

#### \`validate_fields(body: &serde_json::Value) -> Result<LlmRequest, (u16, &'static str, &'static str)>\`
Validates all fields from parsed JSON with fail-fast semantics:
- provider -> missing_provider / invalid_provider
- keyword -> missing_keyword / invalid_keyword (after sanitization)
- location -> missing_location / invalid_location
- language -> invalid_language (if present)
- force_web_search -> invalid_force_web_search / invalid_field_for_provider
- tag -> invalid_tag (if present)

Returns LlmRequest on success or (status_code, error_message, error_code) on failure.

#### \`constant_time_eq(a: &[u8], b: &[u8]) -> bool\`
XOR fold comparison. Same algorithm as rusty-serp. Returns false if lengths differ (known timing leak, accepted tradeoff).

### Wrapper Functions (worker-dependent)

#### \`validate_auth(url: &Url, env: &Env, request_id: &str) -> Result<(), Response>\`
1. Extract csvkey from query params -> 400 missing_csvkey
2. Read CSVKEY secret from env -> 500 missing_config
3. constant_time_eq -> 401 unauthorized

#### \`parse_and_validate_body(req: &mut Request, request_id: &str) -> Result<LlmRequest, Response>\`
1. Read body as text -> 400 missing_body
2. Parse as serde_json::Value -> 400 invalid_json
3. Call validate_fields -> map error tuple to json_error Response

#### \`read_secret(env: &Env, name: &str, request_id: &str) -> Result<String, Response>\`
Read a CF secret. Returns 500 missing_config if absent.

## Validation Rules Detail

| Field | Type Check | Constraints | Error Code |
|-------|-----------|-------------|------------|
| provider | must be string | "gemini", "chatgpt", or "both" (case-insensitive via Provider::from_str) | missing_provider / invalid_provider |
| keyword | must be string | non-empty after sanitization, <= 2000 chars after sanitization | missing_keyword / invalid_keyword |
| location | string or integer | positive integer (> 0) OR non-empty string | missing_location / invalid_location |
| language | string (optional) | 2-5 chars, lowercase alpha only [a-z]; default "en" | invalid_language |
| force_web_search | boolean (optional) | must be JSON boolean (not "true"/1/null). If true and provider is Gemini -> invalid_field_for_provider. If false -> accepted for any provider (no-op). | invalid_force_web_search / invalid_field_for_provider |
| tag | string (optional) | <= 255 chars | invalid_tag |

## Design Decisions

1. **Pure validate_fields function**: The most important testability extraction. ALL validation logic runs on a serde_json::Value with no worker dependencies. This means every validation rule can be exhaustively unit tested.

2. **Fail-fast order**: provider -> keyword -> location -> language -> force_web_search -> tag. Most fundamental error first.

3. **Manual JSON parsing (not serde derive)**: The location field is polymorphic (integer or string). Using serde_json::Value and manual extraction is simpler than a custom deserializer.

4. **Sanitize BEFORE length check**: A keyword of control characters might be under 2000 chars raw but collapse to empty after sanitization.

5. **force_web_search strict validation**: true + Gemini = error. false + Gemini = OK (no-op). true + both = OK (applied to ChatGPT leg only). This prevents agents from getting false confidence that web search was used.

6. **Language validation (2-5 lowercase alpha)**: DataForSEO uses ISO 639-1 codes ("en", "fr", "zh"). Rejects "English" (use "en"), "pt-br" (use "pt"). If too restrictive, relax in v1.1.

## Acceptance
- sanitize_keyword strips control chars, collapses whitespace, trims
- sanitize_keyword("   ") returns "" (empty after trim)
- validate_fields returns correct error for each invalid field
- validate_fields returns LlmRequest with correct types for valid input
- force_web_search: true + gemini -> (400, _, "invalid_field_for_provider")
- force_web_search: false + gemini -> OK
- force_web_search: true + both -> OK

title: Implement src/dataforseo.rs with unit tests — DFS client, request building, auth header
description:
# Task: Implement src/dataforseo.rs — Single Provider Fetch

## What
The DataForSEO client module that builds the upstream request, sends it with an explicit timeout, implements single-retry on transient errors, and handles the response.

## Testability Architecture

Pure core, thin wrapper:
- **Pure function**: build_dfs_task() constructs the JSON task body from an LlmRequest. Fully testable.
- **Pure function**: build_auth_header() constructs the Basic auth string. Fully testable.
- **Thin wrapper**: fetch_llm() uses worker::Fetch to send the request. Tested via E2E only.

## Public Types

### FetchResult
\`\`\`rust
pub struct FetchResult {
    pub response: Response,
    pub retried: bool,
}
\`\`\`

The retried flag propagates back to lib.rs for the X-RustyLLM-Retried header and structured logging. Without this, lib.rs would have no way to know if a retry occurred inside fetch_llm.

## Public API

### Pure Functions (testable)

#### \`build_dfs_task(request: &LlmRequest, request_id: &str) -> serde_json::Value\`
Constructs the DataForSEO task object:
\`\`\`json
{
    "keyword": "...",
    "language_code": "en",
    "location_code": 2840,
    "tag": "request-id-or-user-tag"
}
\`\`\`

Rules:
- location mapped to location_code (integer) or location_name (string) based on LlmRequest::Location variant
- force_web_search included ONLY when provider.supports_force_web_search() AND value is true
- tag set to request.tag if provided, otherwise request_id

#### \`build_auth_header(login: &str, password: &str) -> String\`
Returns: "Basic base64(login:password)"

#### \`wrap_task_array(task: serde_json::Value) -> String\`
Serializes the task inside a JSON array: \`[task]\`. DataForSEO requires the request body to be an array.

### Wrapper Functions (worker-dependent)

#### \`fetch_llm(request: &LlmRequest, login: &str, password: &str, request_id: &str) -> Result<FetchResult, Response>\`

Implementation:
1. Build task via build_dfs_task
2. Build auth via build_auth_header
3. Wrap in array via wrap_task_array
4. Construct worker::Request with POST, headers, body
5. Send with AbortSignal::timeout(120_000)
6. Handle result:
   - AbortError -> 504 dataforseo_timeout
   - Network error -> 502 dataforseo_error with dataforseo_status: 0
   - 429/503 -> retry_once (1 second delay, then re-send)
   - Other non-2xx -> 502 with upstream status + truncated body
   - 2xx -> Ok(FetchResult { response, retried: false })

### Retry Logic

\`\`\`rust
async fn retry_once(/* same params */) -> Result<FetchResult, Response> {
    worker::delay(Duration::from_secs(1)).await;
    // Re-build and re-send the exact same request
    match result {
        Ok(resp) if resp.status_code() >= 200 && resp.status_code() < 300 => {
            Ok(FetchResult { response: resp, retried: true })
        }
        // If retry also fails, return error with retried: true context
        _ => Err(/* error response */)
    }
}
\`\`\`

## Design Decisions

1. **FetchResult struct instead of bare Response**: The retried flag must propagate to lib.rs for headers (X-RustyLLM-Retried) and logging. Returning just Response would lose this information. The struct is cheap (one bool + one Response).

2. **build_dfs_task is pure**: The most important testability extraction. All the conditional logic (location type, force_web_search inclusion, tag defaulting) is in a pure function that can be exhaustively tested.

3. **Explicit AbortSignal vs string-matching**: 120-second timeout via AbortSignal::timeout(120_000) is deterministic. rusty-serp's string-matching on error messages is fragile.

4. **Single retry on 429/503 only**: 429 = rate limited (1s wait usually clears). 503 = temporary unavailability. Other errors (400, 401, 403, 500) are deterministic. No retry on timeout (would double the wait).

5. **1 second retry delay**: Short enough for CF Worker CPU limits. Long enough to back off from rate limiting.

6. **Tag defaults to request_id**: Enables correlation in DataForSEO dashboard. Caller's tag takes precedence if provided.

## Acceptance
- build_dfs_task with Location::Code(2840) -> {"location_code": 2840} (not location_name)
- build_dfs_task with Location::Name("US") -> {"location_name": "US"} (not location_code)
- build_dfs_task with ChatGPT + force_web_search=true -> force_web_search in JSON
- build_dfs_task with ChatGPT + force_web_search=false -> no force_web_search key
- build_dfs_task with Gemini + force_web_search=true -> no force_web_search key (Gemini doesn't support it; validation already prevents this, but belt-and-suspenders)
- build_dfs_task with no tag -> tag = request_id
- build_dfs_task with tag="custom" -> tag = "custom"
- build_auth_header("user", "pass") -> "Basic dXNlcjpwYXNz"
- wrap_task_array produces valid JSON array with one element
- fetch_llm returns FetchResult with retried=false on first-attempt success
- fetch_llm returns FetchResult with retried=true on successful retry
- 429 triggers retry; 503 triggers retry; 400 does not retry
- AbortSignal timeout -> 504
- Network error -> 502 with dataforseo_status: 0

title: Implement src/fetch.rs — fetch_provider, fetch_both, retry logic
description:
# Task: Implement Dual-Provider Fetch ("both" mode)

## What
Extend dataforseo.rs with a fetch_both() function that queries Gemini and ChatGPT concurrently via futures::join!, assembles the combined envelope response, and handles partial failure gracefully.

## Public API

### `fetch_both(request: &LlmRequest, login: &str, password: &str, request_id: &str) -> Result<(Response, bool), Response>`

Returns a tuple: (envelope_response, either_retried). The bool is true if EITHER provider leg triggered a retry. This is needed by lib.rs for the X-RustyLLM-Retried header — we cannot extract it from the Response body without consuming the stream.

## Testability

### Pure Function: `build_envelope(gemini: ProviderOutcome, chatgpt: ProviderOutcome, request_id: &str, total_duration: u64) -> (u16, serde_json::Value, bool)`

Takes the outcome of each provider fetch and constructs the envelope JSON + HTTP status code + combined retried flag. Fully testable.

```rust
pub struct ProviderOutcome {
    pub status: &'static str,    // "ok" or "error"
    pub duration_ms: u64,
    pub model: Option<String>,
    pub dfs_cost_cents: Option<u64>,
    pub response_body: Option<serde_json::Value>,  // On success
    pub error: Option<String>,                      // On failure
    pub code: Option<String>,                       // On failure
    pub dataforseo_status: Option<u16>,             // On failure
    pub dataforseo_body: Option<String>,            // On failure
    pub retried: bool,
}
```

Returns: (http_status, envelope_json, either_retried)
- (200, envelope, retried) if both status == "ok"
- (207, envelope, retried) if one "ok" and one "error"
- (502, envelope, retried) if both "error"
- retried = gemini.retried || chatgpt.retried

## Concurrent Execution

```rust
// Create provider-specific versions of the request
let gemini_request = LlmRequest {
    provider: Provider::Gemini,
    ..request.clone()
};
let chatgpt_request = LlmRequest {
    provider: Provider::ChatGpt,
    force_web_search: request.force_web_search, // Only applied to ChatGPT leg via build_dfs_task
    ..request.clone()
};

let (gemini_result, chatgpt_result) = futures::join!(
    fetch_single_for_both(&gemini_request, login, password, request_id),
    fetch_single_for_both(&chatgpt_request, login, password, request_id),
);
```

Note: LlmRequest must derive Clone for this pattern. Add `#[derive(Clone)]` to the struct in validation.rs (bead 2.3).

fetch_single_for_both is a thin wrapper around the existing single-provider logic that captures timing and returns ProviderOutcome instead of FetchResult.

## Envelope Structure

### Both succeed (200):
```json
{
  "request_id": "...",
  "provider": "both",
  "duration_ms": 8700,
  "gemini": {
    "status": "ok",
    "duration_ms": 4200,
    "model": "gemini-2.0-flash",
    "dfs_cost_cents": 40,
    "response": { /* verbatim DataForSEO response */ }
  },
  "chatgpt": {
    "status": "ok",
    "duration_ms": 8700,
    "model": "gpt-4o",
    "dfs_cost_cents": 40,
    "response": { /* verbatim DataForSEO response */ }
  }
}
```

### One fails (207):
```json
{
  "request_id": "...",
  "provider": "both",
  "duration_ms": 12100,
  "gemini": { "status": "ok", ... },
  "chatgpt": {
    "status": "error",
    "duration_ms": 12100,
    "error": "DataForSEO returned non-success status",
    "code": "dataforseo_error",
    "dataforseo_status": 500,
    "dataforseo_body": "..."
  }
}
```

### Both fail (502):
Same structure, both entries have "status": "error".

## Design Decisions

1. **futures::join! for concurrency**: CF Workers support concurrent async on a single thread. Total latency = max(gemini, chatgpt), not sum. This is THE value proposition of "both" mode.

2. **Independent retry per provider**: Each leg has its own retry. Gemini 429 + ChatGPT success = only Gemini retries.

3. **207 Multi-Status for partial failure**: Semantically correct HTTP status. Agents check the status field within each entry.

4. **502 only when BOTH fail**: If at least one returned useful data, the request was partially successful.

5. **force_web_search in "both" mode**: Gemini leg omits it (Provider::Gemini.supports_force_web_search() == false). ChatGPT leg includes it if set. Validation permits force_web_search with "both" provider.

6. **ProviderOutcome struct**: Enables testing build_envelope with mock outcomes. No need to actually call DataForSEO to test envelope construction logic.

7. **Envelope intentionally breaks pass-through**: This is the ONE case where DataForSEO responses are not returned verbatim at the top level. Individual response fields within the envelope ARE still verbatim. The comparison value justifies the schema coupling.

8. **Return (Response, bool) not bare Response**: The caller needs the retried flag for headers. Reading it from the JSON body would consume the response stream. Returning a tuple is the clean solution — same pattern as FetchResult but for dual-provider.

9. **LlmRequest must derive Clone**: Needed to create per-provider copies. Added to 2.3 acceptance criteria.

## Acceptance
- Both succeed: HTTP 200, both entries have status "ok" and response data
- One fails: HTTP 207, one "ok" with response, one "error" with details
- Both fail: HTTP 502, both entries have "error" details
- build_envelope(ok, ok) returns (200, ..., retried)
- build_envelope(ok, err) returns (207, ..., retried)
- build_envelope(err, err) returns (502, ..., retried)
- Return tuple: (Response, true) when either leg retried, (Response, false) otherwise
- Concurrent execution verified via timing: total ~= max(gemini, chatgpt), NOT sum
- force_web_search only in ChatGPT leg body
- Retry logic independent per provider
- envelope includes request_id and total duration_ms
- Per-provider entries include individual duration_ms, model, cost
close_reason: fetch_both() implemented in dataforseo.rs using futures::join! for concurrent dual-provider fetches. Returns (Response, bool) tuple. fetch_single_for_both wraps fetch_llm and produces ProviderOutcome. WASM target compiles clean.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: BUG FIX: Changed fetch_both return type from Result<Response, Response> to Result<(Response, bool), Response>. The bool indicates whether either provider was retried. Reason: the caller (handle_llm in 3.3) needs the retried flag for the X-RustyLLM-Retried header, but cannot read it from the response body without consuming the stream. The function already has this info internally from both ProviderOutcome.retried fields — just bubble it up as a tuple.

description:
# Phase 3: Orchestration, Observability & Unit Tests (lib.rs)

## Purpose
Wire everything together in lib.rs: routing, request ID generation, the handle_llm() pipeline, metadata header attachment, structured logging, and the health check endpoint. Then validate all orchestration logic via unit tests.

## Why After Phase 2
lib.rs is pure orchestration — it calls into validation.rs and dataforseo.rs but contains no business logic itself. It can only be written once those modules exist.

## Key Responsibilities of lib.rs
1. Generate UUID v4 request_id at the top of every request
2. Route by (Method, Path) — health check, llm endpoint, 404/405
3. Call validation pipeline (auth → parse → validate)
4. Read secrets from CF environment
5. Dispatch to dataforseo (single or dual provider)
6. Attach metadata response headers (shallow-parsed from DFS response)
7. Emit one structured JSON log line per request
8. Return response (pass-through body + headers, or error with request_id)

## Design Constraint
The handle_llm function uses the same async-block-returning-Result pattern as rusty-serp. The "happy path" is a chain of `?` operators. Any failure short-circuits and returns the error Response directly.

## Testing (Bead 3.6)
Unit tests for lib.rs cover pure functions extracted from the orchestration layer:
- UUID v4 format validation (regex match, uniqueness across calls)
- Health check response structure (JSON body, correct fields)
- Metadata header extraction from mock DFS JSON
- Log line structure validation (all required fields present)

These tests use the "pure core" pattern — they test the logic functions that lib.rs calls, NOT the worker SDK wrappers.

title: Implement UUID v4 generation for request IDs
description:
# Task: Implement UUID v4 Generation

## What
Create a lightweight UUID v4 generator that runs in WASM using the `getrandom` crate (which delegates to `crypto.getRandomValues()` in the browser/worker via the `js` feature).

## Implementation

Place in `src/lib.rs` as a private helper function:

```rust
fn generate_request_id() -> String {
    let mut bytes = [0u8; 16];
    getrandom::getrandom(&mut bytes).expect("getrandom failed");
    
    // Set version (4) and variant (RFC 4122) bits
    bytes[6] = (bytes[6] & 0x0f) | 0x40; // Version 4
    bytes[8] = (bytes[8] & 0x3f) | 0x80; // Variant 1
    
    format!(
        "{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}-{:02x}{:02x}-{:02x}{:02x}-{:02x}{:02x}-{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}",
        bytes[0], bytes[1], bytes[2], bytes[3],
        bytes[4], bytes[5],
        bytes[6], bytes[7],
        bytes[8], bytes[9],
        bytes[10], bytes[11], bytes[12], bytes[13], bytes[14], bytes[15]
    )
}
```

## Why Not Use a UUID Crate?
- Adding `uuid` crate would increase binary size by ~20-40KB
- We only need UUID v4 (random), which is trivial to implement
- `getrandom` is already a dependency (needed for the `js` randomness source)
- The 15-line implementation above is simpler than managing another dependency

## Why `getrandom` with `js` Feature?
WASM has no native randomness source. The `js` feature makes `getrandom` delegate to `crypto.getRandomValues()` which is available in the CF Workers runtime. This is cryptographically secure randomness — appropriate for request IDs that might appear in logs and need to be unguessable.

## Considerations
- The `expect("getrandom failed")` will panic if crypto.getRandomValues is somehow unavailable. This should never happen in CF Workers but would be caught by console_error_panic_hook.
- UUID format is lowercase hex with hyphens (standard RFC 4122 format)
- Generated at the very top of the request handler, before any other processing

## Acceptance
- Generated IDs are valid UUID v4 format (regex: `^[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-4[0-9a-f]{3}-[89ab][0-9a-f]{3}-[0-9a-f]{12}$`)
- Two consecutive calls produce different IDs
- Works in WASM/CF Workers environment

title: Implement request routing in lib.rs (health, llm, 404, 405)
description:
# Task: Implement Request Routing

## What
Replace the scaffold 404-for-everything with proper method+path routing in lib.rs.

## Route Table

| Method | Path | Handler |
|--------|------|---------|
| GET | /v1/health | handle_health(env) |
| HEAD | /v1/health | handle_health_head(env) |
| POST | /v1/llm | handle_llm() |
| * | /v1/health | 405 method_not_allowed |
| * | /v1/llm | 405 method_not_allowed |
| * | * | 404 not_found |

## Implementation Pattern

\`\`\`rust
#[event(fetch)]
async fn fetch(req: Request, env: Env, _ctx: Context) -> Result<Response> {
    console_error_panic_hook::set_once();

    let path = req.path();
    let method = req.method();

    match (method, path.as_str()) {
        (Method::Get, "/v1/health") => handle_health(&env),
        (Method::Head, "/v1/health") => handle_health_head(&env),
        (Method::Post, "/v1/llm") => {
            let request_id = generate_request_id();
            Ok(handle_llm(req, env, &request_id).await.unwrap_or_else(|e| e))
        }
        (_, "/v1/health") | (_, "/v1/llm") => {
            Ok(json_error(405, "Method not allowed", "method_not_allowed", ""))
        }
        _ => {
            Ok(json_error(404, "Not found", "not_found", ""))
        }
    }
}
\`\`\`

## Health Check Response

\`\`\`rust
fn handle_health(env: &Env) -> Result<Response> {
    let secrets_ok = env.secret("CSVKEY").is_ok()
        && env.secret("DATAFORSEO_LOGIN").is_ok()
        && env.secret("DATAFORSEO_PASSWORD").is_ok();

    Response::from_json(&json!({
        "status": "ok",
        "version": env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"),
        "providers": ["gemini", "chatgpt", "both"],
        "secrets_configured": secrets_ok
    }))
}
\`\`\`

### Why secrets_configured?
The most common deployment failure is forgetting to set secrets after deploying. Without this field, the first agent request fails with a cryptic 500 missing_config. With it, a health check immediately reveals the problem. The boolean reveals NO information about secret values or even which specific secret is missing — only whether all three are set.

### Version from Cargo.toml
Uses \`env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION")\` to read the version at compile time from Cargo.toml. No manual version string maintenance.

## Design Decisions

1. **Match on (Method, Path) tuple**: Clean, exhaustive, no router library needed. Same as rusty-serp.

2. **Request ID generated before handle_llm**: The ID exists from the very start of request processing, so even early validation errors include it.

3. **unwrap_or_else(|e| e) pattern**: handle_llm returns Result<Response, Response>. Both Ok and Err are valid HTTP responses. This collapses the Result into a Response.

4. **No request_id for 404/405**: Routing errors don't represent meaningful agent requests. Empty string request_id is fine.

5. **Health check passes env**: Needs access to secrets for the secrets_configured check.

6. **HEAD returns headers only**: Standard HTTP — HEAD is identical to GET but with no body. Important for uptime monitoring probes that want minimal bandwidth.

## Acceptance
- GET /v1/health -> 200 with JSON body including status, version, providers, secrets_configured
- GET /v1/health with secrets set -> secrets_configured: true
- GET /v1/health without secrets -> secrets_configured: false
- HEAD /v1/health -> 200 with headers, no body
- POST /v1/llm -> delegates to handle_llm
- PUT /v1/llm -> 405
- GET /v1/llm -> 405
- GET /anything-else -> 404
- All responses have Content-Type: application/json
- Version matches Cargo.toml version string

title: Implement structured JSON logging (one line per request)
description:
# Task: Implement Structured Logging

## What
Emit one JSON log line per request via `console_log!`. This is captured by CF Workers' real-time logging (`wrangler tail`) and dashboard.

## Log Format

```json
{
  "req_id": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890",
  "provider": "gemini",
  "keyword_len": 42,
  "location": "2840",
  "duration_ms": 4200,
  "status": 200,
  "dfs_status": 20000,
  "retried": false
}
```

## Fields

| Field | Source | Purpose |
|-------|--------|---------|
| req_id | Generated UUID | Correlate with agent's X-RustyLLM-Request-Id header |
| provider | From validated request | Know which endpoint was called |
| keyword_len | keyword.len() | Debugging without logging PII/sensitive keywords |
| location | Stringified (code as number, name as string) | Geographic context |
| duration_ms | Elapsed time | Performance monitoring |
| status | HTTP status returned to agent | Outcome classification |
| dfs_status | DataForSEO task status_code | Upstream health monitoring |
| retried | Whether retry was triggered | Track transient failure frequency |

## Implementation

```rust
fn emit_log(
    request_id: &str,
    request: &LlmRequest,
    duration_ms: u64,
    status: u16,
    dfs_status: Option<i64>,
    retried: bool,
) {
    let location_str = match &request.location {
        Location::Code(c) => c.to_string(),
        Location::Name(n) => n.clone(),
    };
    
    console_log!(
        "{{\"req_id\":\"{}\",\"provider\":\"{}\",\"keyword_len\":{},\"location\":\"{}\",\"duration_ms\":{},\"status\":{},\"dfs_status\":{},\"retried\":{}}}",
        request_id,
        request.provider.name(),
        request.keyword.len(),
        location_str,
        duration_ms,
        status,
        dfs_status.map_or("null".to_string(), |s| s.to_string()),
        retried
    );
}
```

## Design Decisions

1. **keyword_len not keyword**: Keywords could contain sensitive brand names, competitor names, or proprietary queries. Logging the length gives enough info for debugging ("was this a huge keyword that caused a timeout?") without leaking content.

2. **String-formatted JSON (not serde)**: Using format! with manual JSON construction avoids allocating a serde_json::Value just to log it. The fields are all simple types — no escaping concerns for UUIDs, numbers, and pre-validated strings.

3. **Single line per request**: CF Workers logging truncates multi-line output. One JSON line per request ensures the full log entry is captured.

4. **Log on EVERY request including errors**: Even 400 validation errors get logged. This enables tracking attack patterns (repeated invalid csvkeys) and validation issues.

5. **dfs_status can be null**: If the request fails before reaching DataForSEO (auth failure, validation error), dfs_status is null. This distinguishes "never called DataForSEO" from "DataForSEO returned an error."

## Acceptance
- Every request to /v1/llm produces exactly one log line
- Log line is valid JSON
- keyword content is NOT in logs (only length)
- `wrangler tail` shows the log lines in real time
- Error requests still produce log lines (with appropriate null values)

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: BOTH MODE LOGGING: In "both" mode, the structured log line uses provider="both", dfs_status=null (since there are two upstream statuses, they live in the envelope body not the log). The individual per-provider durations, models, and DFS statuses are in the JSON envelope response. The log captures the aggregate: total duration, overall HTTP status (200/207/502), and whether either leg was retried.

title: Unit tests for lib.rs — UUID v4, health check, and extract_metadata

description:
# Phase 4: Testing, Verification & Deployment

## Purpose
Validate all functionality through three layers: (1) cargo test + clippy gate ensuring all unit tests pass and code is lint-free, (2) manual curl tests against a deployed worker covering every endpoint and error case, and (3) an automated E2E test script for repeatable regression testing. Then promote through environments.

## Why This Order Matters
1. **Cargo test gate (4.9)** runs FIRST — catches logic regressions before deployment
2. **Manual tests (4.1–4.5)** validate real behavior against live DataForSEO APIs
3. **E2E script (4.8)** codifies the manual tests for future CI integration
4. **Deployment (4.6)** promotes to staging/prod only after all tests pass
5. **README (4.7)** documents the final validated API

## Test Coverage Required
- Happy path: Gemini, ChatGPT, and "both" modes
- Auth: missing key, wrong key
- Validation: every error code in the taxonomy
- Provider rules: force_web_search + gemini → 400
- Upstream failures: timeout behavior, non-2xx passthrough
- Observability: request_id in headers and error bodies, metadata headers present
- Dual-mode: partial failure (207), both fail (502)

## Automated E2E Script (Bead 4.8)
A self-contained bash script (tests/e2e.sh) that:
- Sources .env for API keys and worker URL
- Runs every test case with detailed pass/fail logging
- Validates response status codes, JSON structure, and header presence
- Produces a summary report at the end
- Exits non-zero on any failure (CI-compatible)

## Cargo Test Gate (Bead 4.9)
Before any E2E testing, run:
- `cargo test` — all unit tests must pass
- `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` — zero warnings policy
This catches pure-logic regressions before slower integration tests run.

## Deployment Sequence
1. Deploy to dev (bare `wrangler deploy`) → full curl test suite
2. Deploy to staging (`--env staging`) → smoke test
3. Deploy to production (`--env production`) → smoke test
4. Document in README with working curl examples

title: Manual test: dual-provider mode ("both") including partial failure
description:
# Task: Test Dual-Provider Mode (via E2E script + manual verification)

## What
Validate the "both" mode returns a combined envelope with concurrent results, verify partial failure handling, and confirm concurrent execution via timing analysis.

## Steps

### 1. Run E2E script (both section)
\`\`\`bash
./tests/e2e.sh --url "https://rusty-llm.<subdomain>.workers.dev" --csvkey "<key>" --section both
\`\`\`

### 2. Manual inspection of dual-provider response
\`\`\`bash
curl -s -D /dev/stderr -X POST "...?csvkey=<key>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"provider":"both","keyword":"best CRM software for startups","location":2840,"language":"en","force_web_search":true}' | jq .
\`\`\`

Verify envelope structure:
- Top-level: request_id, provider="both", duration_ms
- gemini.status = "ok"
- gemini.duration_ms (number)
- gemini.model (Gemini model name)
- gemini.dfs_cost_cents (number)
- gemini.response (full DataForSEO Gemini response object)
- chatgpt.status = "ok"
- chatgpt.duration_ms (number)
- chatgpt.model (GPT model name)
- chatgpt.dfs_cost_cents (number)
- chatgpt.response (full DataForSEO ChatGPT response object)

### 3. Concurrency Verification (CRITICAL)
This is the key validation that futures::join! is working:

\`\`\`bash
# Extract timings
RESPONSE=$(curl -s -X POST "...?csvkey=<key>" -d '{"provider":"both","keyword":"test","location":2840}')
GEMINI_MS=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | jq '.gemini.duration_ms')
CHATGPT_MS=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | jq '.chatgpt.duration_ms')
TOTAL_MS=$(echo "$RESPONSE" | jq '.duration_ms')

echo "Gemini: ${GEMINI_MS}ms, ChatGPT: ${CHATGPT_MS}ms, Total: ${TOTAL_MS}ms"
echo "Sum would be: $((GEMINI_MS + CHATGPT_MS))ms"
echo "Concurrency savings: $((GEMINI_MS + CHATGPT_MS - TOTAL_MS))ms"
\`\`\`

Expected: total_ms ≈ max(gemini_ms, chatgpt_ms), NOT sum.
- If concurrent: total ~8-15s (typical max of the two)
- If sequential: total ~12-25s (sum of both)
- Threshold: total should be < 1.3 * max(gemini, chatgpt) to confirm concurrency

### 4. force_web_search asymmetry
Verify that force_web_search is applied to ChatGPT leg but NOT Gemini:
- chatgpt.response.tasks[0].data should show force_web_search in the echoed params
- gemini.response.tasks[0].data should NOT show force_web_search

### 5. Testing Partial Failure (207)
This is hard to reliably trigger against real DataForSEO. Options:
- Use an obscure language code that one provider might reject
- Accept this is primarily validated via unit tests (build_envelope tests cover the 207 logic)
- If 207 IS observed during testing, verify the structure matches spec

## Verification Checklist
- [ ] E2E script both section: all PASS
- [ ] Envelope structure matches spec (request_id, provider, duration_ms, gemini, chatgpt)
- [ ] Both providers return valid response data
- [ ] Concurrency confirmed: total_ms < 1.3 * max(gemini_ms, chatgpt_ms)
- [ ] force_web_search appears in chatgpt data echo but not gemini
- [ ] X-RustyLLM-Provider header = "both"
- [ ] X-RustyLLM-Duration-Ms matches envelope's duration_ms

#### Sanitization edge case:
\`\`\`bash
# Keyword with only control chars — should fail (empty after sanitization)
curl -s -X POST "...?csvkey=<key>" \
  -d '{"provider":"gemini","keyword":"\\u0001\\u0002\\u0003","location":2840}' | jq .
# Expected: 400, code: invalid_keyword

  -d '{"provider":"gemini","keyword":"test","location":2840,"force_web_search":false}' | jq .tasks[0].status_code

  -d '{"provider":"both","keyword":"test","location":2840,"force_web_search":true}' | jq .chatgpt.status

#### Location = 0 (boundary, should fail):

  -d '{"provider":"gemini","keyword":"test","location":0}' | jq .code
# Expected: "invalid_location"
\`\`\`

#### Fail-fast ordering (provider error reported before keyword error):
\`\`\`bash

  -d '{"provider":"invalid","keyword":"","location":0}' | jq .code
# Expected: "invalid_provider" (not "invalid_keyword" — provider checked first)

### 3. Verify error response structure consistently
For each error, confirm:
- Correct HTTP status code
- JSON body has: request_id, error, code

- Content-Type: application/json

title: Manual test: observability (request IDs, headers, logging)
description:
# Task: Test Observability Features (via E2E script + manual verification)

## What
Verify the full observability system works end-to-end: request IDs unique and consistent, metadata headers populated, structured logs appear in wrangler tail, and error responses include IDs.

## Steps

### 1. Run E2E script (observability section)
\`\`\`bash
./tests/e2e.sh --url "https://rusty-llm.<subdomain>.workers.dev" --csvkey "<key>" --section observability
\`\`\`

### 2. Request ID deep verification
\`\`\`bash
# Extract request ID from a successful response
RESP_HEADERS=$(curl -s -D - -X POST "...valid..." -o /tmp/e2e-body.json)
REQ_ID=$(echo "$RESP_HEADERS" | grep -i "X-RustyLLM-Request-Id" | awk '{print $2}' | tr -d '\r')

# Verify UUID v4 format
echo "$REQ_ID" | grep -qE '^[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-4[0-9a-f]{3}-[89ab][0-9a-f]{3}-[0-9a-f]{12}$'
echo "Valid UUID v4: $?"

# Verify uniqueness (make 5 rapid requests)
for i in {1..5}; do
  curl -s -D - -X POST "...valid..." -o /dev/null 2>&1 | grep X-RustyLLM-Request-Id
done
# All 5 should be different
\`\`\`

### 3. Request ID consistency (header = body in errors)
\`\`\`bash
# Make an error request
RESP=$(curl -s -D /tmp/err-headers.txt -X POST "...?csvkey=wrong" \
  -d '{"provider":"gemini","keyword":"test","location":2840}')

# Get ID from header
HEADER_ID=$(grep -i "X-RustyLLM-Request-Id" /tmp/err-headers.txt | awk '{print $2}' | tr -d '\r')
# Get ID from body
BODY_ID=$(echo "$RESP" | jq -r '.request_id')

# They must match
echo "Header: $HEADER_ID"
echo "Body:   $BODY_ID"
[ "$HEADER_ID" = "$BODY_ID" ] && echo "MATCH" || echo "MISMATCH!"
\`\`\`

### 4. Metadata headers completeness
\`\`\`bash
# Success response should have all 7 headers
curl -s -D - -X POST "...valid gemini..." -o /dev/null 2>&1 | grep "X-RustyLLM" | sort

# Expected output (all 7):
# X-RustyLLM-DFS-Cost-Cents: 40
# X-RustyLLM-DFS-Status: 20000
# X-RustyLLM-Duration-Ms: 4200
# X-RustyLLM-Model: gemini-2.0-flash
# X-RustyLLM-Provider: gemini
# X-RustyLLM-Request-Id: a1b2c3d4-...
# X-RustyLLM-Retried: false
\`\`\`

### 5. Metadata header values sanity
- Duration-Ms: > 0 and < 120000 (not negative, not over timeout)
- DFS-Cost-Cents: > 0 (usually 40 = $0.004)
- DFS-Status: 20000 (DataForSEO success code)
- Model: non-empty string starting with "gemini" or "gpt"
- Retried: "true" or "false" (not other values)

### 6. Structured logging (wrangler tail)
\`\`\`bash
# In one terminal:
wrangler tail --format json 2>&1 | tee /tmp/tail-output.txt &

# In another terminal: make a request
curl -s -X POST "...valid gemini..." -o /dev/null

# Check tail output for structured log line
cat /tmp/tail-output.txt | jq 'select(.logs[].message[0] | contains("req_id"))' | head -5
\`\`\`

Verify log line contains:
- req_id (matches the X-RustyLLM-Request-Id from the response)
- provider: "gemini"
- keyword_len: (positive integer, NOT the actual keyword)
- location: "2840"
- duration_ms: (positive integer)
- status: 200
- dfs_status: 20000
- retried: false

### 7. Error request logging
Make a 400 error request and verify it ALSO produces a log line (with status: 400, dfs_status: null).

## Verification Checklist
- [ ] E2E script observability section: all 5 PASS
- [ ] Request IDs are valid UUID v4 format
- [ ] Request IDs are unique across 5 rapid requests
- [ ] Request ID in header matches request_id in error body
- [ ] All 7 metadata headers present on single-provider 200
- [ ] Header values are sane and within expected ranges
- [ ] Structured log line appears in wrangler tail
- [ ] Log line is valid JSON with all expected fields
- [ ] Error requests also produce log lines
- [ ] Log does NOT contain the actual keyword text (privacy)
- [ ] "both" mode has X-RustyLLM-Request-Id, Provider, Duration-Ms, Retried headers

title: Deploy to staging and production environments

# Task: Deploy to Staging and Production

## What
After all dev testing passes, promote the worker through staging and production with smoke tests at each level.

## Deployment Steps

### Staging
```bash
# Set staging secrets
wrangler secret put CSVKEY --env staging
wrangler secret put DATAFORSEO_LOGIN --env staging
wrangler secret put DATAFORSEO_PASSWORD --env staging

# Deploy
wrangler deploy --env staging

# Smoke test
curl -s -X POST "https://rusty-llm-staging.<subdomain>.workers.dev/v1/llm?csvkey=<staging-key>" \
  -d '{"provider":"gemini","keyword":"test","location":2840}'
# Verify 200 + valid response

curl -s "https://rusty-llm-staging.<subdomain>.workers.dev/v1/health"
# Verify health check response
```

### Production
```bash
# Set production secrets
wrangler secret put CSVKEY --env production
wrangler secret put DATAFORSEO_LOGIN --env production
wrangler secret put DATAFORSEO_PASSWORD --env production

# Deploy
wrangler deploy --env production

# Smoke test (all three modes)
curl -s -X POST "https://rusty-llm.<subdomain>.workers.dev/v1/llm?csvkey=<prod-key>" \
  -d '{"provider":"gemini","keyword":"test","location":2840}'

curl -s -X POST "https://rusty-llm.<subdomain>.workers.dev/v1/llm?csvkey=<prod-key>" \
  -d '{"provider":"chatgpt","keyword":"test","location":2840}'

curl -s -X POST "https://rusty-llm.<subdomain>.workers.dev/v1/llm?csvkey=<prod-key>" \
  -d '{"provider":"both","keyword":"test","location":2840}'
```

## Secret Strategy
- Use DIFFERENT csvkey values for staging vs production
- Use the SAME DataForSEO credentials (same account, costs are tracked via tag)
- Production csvkey should be shared with agent teams after deployment

## Verification
- [ ] Staging deployment successful
- [ ] Staging smoke test passes (Gemini)
- [ ] Production deployment successful
- [ ] Production smoke test passes (Gemini)
- [ ] Production smoke test passes (ChatGPT)
- [ ] Production smoke test passes (both)
- [ ] Binary size verified < 500KB in deployment logs
- [ ] wrangler tail --env production shows structured logs

## Rollback Plan
If production deployment has issues:
```bash
# Revert to previous version via CF dashboard
# OR deploy known-good commit:
git checkout <last-good-sha> && wrangler deploy --env production
```

## Post-Deployment
- Share production URL and csvkey with agent teams
- Document the worker URL in relevant agent configs
- Add to uptime monitoring (if infrastructure supports it)

title: Create automated E2E test script (tests/e2e.sh) with detailed logging
description:
# Task: Create Automated E2E Test Script

## What
A comprehensive bash script that exercises every endpoint, every provider mode, every error case, and every observability feature against a deployed worker. Outputs structured, color-coded results with detailed logging. Exits with the count of failures.

## Location
\`tests/e2e.sh\`

## Usage
\`\`\`bash
# Required arguments
./tests/e2e.sh --url "https://rusty-llm.<subdomain>.workers.dev" --csvkey "your-key"

# Optional: verbose mode (prints full response bodies)
./tests/e2e.sh --url "..." --csvkey "..." --verbose

# Optional: run only a specific test section
./tests/e2e.sh --url "..." --csvkey "..." --section health
./tests/e2e.sh --url "..." --csvkey "..." --section gemini
./tests/e2e.sh --url "..." --csvkey "..." --section chatgpt
./tests/e2e.sh --url "..." --csvkey "..." --section both
./tests/e2e.sh --url "..." --csvkey "..." --section errors
./tests/e2e.sh --url "..." --csvkey "..." --section observability
\`\`\`

## Script Architecture

### Test Framework Functions
\`\`\`bash
PASS=0
FAIL=0
SKIP=0
LOG_FILE="tests/e2e-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).log"

test_case() {
    local name="$1"
    local expected_status="$2"
    local method="$3"
    local path="$4"
    local body="$5"  # Optional
    local extra_checks="$6"  # Optional function name for additional validation

    echo -n "  [$name] "
    # Full curl with headers captured
    local response_file=$(mktemp)
    local header_file=$(mktemp)
    local http_status

    if [ -n "$body" ]; then
        http_status=$(curl -s -o "$response_file" -D "$header_file" \
            -w "%{http_code}" -X "$method" \
            "${BASE_URL}${path}" \
            -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
            -d "$body")
    else
        http_status=$(curl -s -o "$response_file" -D "$header_file" \
            -w "%{http_code}" -X "$method" \
            "${BASE_URL}${path}")
    fi

    # Log full details
    echo "=== TEST: $name ===" >> "$LOG_FILE"
    echo "Request: $method ${BASE_URL}${path}" >> "$LOG_FILE"
    [ -n "$body" ] && echo "Body: $body" >> "$LOG_FILE"
    echo "Expected status: $expected_status" >> "$LOG_FILE"
    echo "Actual status: $http_status" >> "$LOG_FILE"
    echo "Response headers:" >> "$LOG_FILE"
    cat "$header_file" >> "$LOG_FILE"
    echo "Response body:" >> "$LOG_FILE"
    cat "$response_file" >> "$LOG_FILE"
    echo "" >> "$LOG_FILE"

    # Status check
    if [ "$http_status" != "$expected_status" ]; then
        echo -e "\033[31mFAIL\033[0m (expected $expected_status, got $http_status)"
        [ "$VERBOSE" = true ] && cat "$response_file" | jq . 2>/dev/null
        FAIL=$((FAIL + 1))
        rm -f "$response_file" "$header_file"
        return 1
    fi

    # Run extra checks if provided
    if [ -n "$extra_checks" ]; then
        if ! "$extra_checks" "$response_file" "$header_file"; then
            echo -e "\033[31mFAIL\033[0m (extra check failed)"
            FAIL=$((FAIL + 1))
            rm -f "$response_file" "$header_file"
            return 1
        fi
    fi

    echo -e "\033[32mPASS\033[0m"
    PASS=$((PASS + 1))
    rm -f "$response_file" "$header_file"
    return 0
}
\`\`\`

## Test Sections

### Section 1: Health Check (3 tests)
\`\`\`
health_get           — GET /v1/health -> 200, body has status/version/providers/secrets_configured
health_head          — HEAD /v1/health -> 200, no body
health_post          — POST /v1/health -> 405
\`\`\`

### Section 2: Gemini Provider (4 tests)
\`\`\`
gemini_basic         — Basic Gemini request -> 200, valid DFS response
gemini_location_name — Location as string -> 200
gemini_defaults      — Omit optional fields -> 200 (language defaults to "en")
gemini_custom_tag    — With tag -> 200, verify tag in DFS response data
\`\`\`

### Section 3: ChatGPT Provider (3 tests)
\`\`\`
chatgpt_basic        — Basic ChatGPT request -> 200
chatgpt_web_search   — With force_web_search: true -> 200
chatgpt_no_search    — Without force_web_search -> 200
\`\`\`

### Section 4: Dual Provider "both" (3 tests)
\`\`\`
both_basic           — Both mode -> 200, envelope structure with gemini + chatgpt
both_web_search      — Both + force_web_search -> 200, applied to chatgpt leg only
both_concurrency     — Verify duration_ms ~= max(gemini, chatgpt), NOT sum
\`\`\`

### Section 5: Error Cases (16 tests)
\`\`\`
err_missing_csvkey        — No csvkey -> 400, code: missing_csvkey
err_wrong_csvkey          — Wrong csvkey -> 401, code: unauthorized
err_empty_body            — Empty body -> 400, code: missing_body
err_invalid_json          — Invalid JSON -> 400, code: invalid_json
err_missing_provider      — No provider -> 400, code: missing_provider
err_invalid_provider      — Bad provider -> 400, code: invalid_provider
err_missing_keyword       — No keyword -> 400, code: missing_keyword
err_empty_keyword         — Whitespace-only keyword -> 400, code: invalid_keyword
err_long_keyword          — 2001-char keyword -> 400, code: invalid_keyword
err_missing_location      — No location -> 400, code: missing_location
err_negative_location     — Negative integer -> 400, code: invalid_location
err_empty_location        — Empty string -> 400, code: invalid_location
err_bad_language          — "English" -> 400, code: invalid_language
err_force_ws_gemini       — force_web_search:true + gemini -> 400, code: invalid_field_for_provider
err_force_ws_not_bool     — force_web_search:"true" -> 400, code: invalid_force_web_search
err_tag_too_long          — 256-char tag -> 400, code: invalid_tag
\`\`\`

### Section 6: Routing Errors (3 tests)
\`\`\`
route_404                 — GET /v1/serp -> 404
route_405_get_llm         — GET /v1/llm -> 405
route_405_put_llm         — PUT /v1/llm -> 405
\`\`\`

### Section 7: Observability (5 tests)
\`\`\`
obs_request_id_format     — X-RustyLLM-Request-Id is valid UUID v4
obs_request_id_unique     — Two requests have different IDs
obs_request_id_in_error   — Error response JSON contains request_id matching header
obs_metadata_headers      — All 7 metadata headers present on 200
obs_metadata_values       — Header values are sane (duration > 0, dfs_status = 20000, etc.)
\`\`\`

## Validation Helpers

\`\`\`bash
check_json_field() {
    local file="$1"
    local field="$2"
    local expected="$3"
    local actual=$(jq -r "$field" "$file" 2>/dev/null)
    if [ "$actual" != "$expected" ]; then
        echo "    Expected $field = '$expected', got '$actual'" >> "$LOG_FILE"
        return 1
    fi
}

check_header() {
    local header_file="$1"
    local header_name="$2"
    local value=$(grep -i "^$header_name:" "$header_file" | awk '{print $2}' | tr -d '\r')
    if [ -z "$value" ]; then
        echo "    Header $header_name missing" >> "$LOG_FILE"
        return 1
    fi
    echo "$value"
}

check_uuid_v4() {
    local id="$1"
    if echo "$id" | grep -qE '^[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-4[0-9a-f]{3}-[89ab][0-9a-f]{3}-[0-9a-f]{12}$'; then
        return 0
    fi
    echo "    Not valid UUID v4: $id" >> "$LOG_FILE"
    return 1
}
\`\`\`

## Output Format

\`\`\`
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║     rusty-llm E2E Test Suite                 ║
║     URL: https://rusty-llm.xxx.workers.dev   ║
║     Time: 2026-05-25 21:00:00 UTC           ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════╝

[Health Check]
  [health_get]           PASS
  [health_head]          PASS
  [health_post]          PASS

[Gemini Provider]
  [gemini_basic]         PASS
  [gemini_location_name] PASS
  [gemini_defaults]      PASS
  [gemini_custom_tag]    PASS

[ChatGPT Provider]
  [chatgpt_basic]        PASS
  [chatgpt_web_search]   PASS
  [chatgpt_no_search]    PASS

[Dual Provider "both"]
  [both_basic]           PASS
  [both_web_search]      PASS
  [both_concurrency]     PASS

[Error Cases]
  [err_missing_csvkey]   PASS
  ... (16 tests)

[Routing]
  [route_404]            PASS
  [route_405_get_llm]    PASS
  [route_405_put_llm]    PASS

[Observability]
  [obs_request_id_format]  PASS
  [obs_request_id_unique]  PASS
  [obs_metadata_headers]   PASS
  [obs_metadata_values]    PASS
  [obs_request_id_in_error] PASS

══════════════════════════════════════════════
  RESULTS: 37 passed, 0 failed, 0 skipped
  Log file: tests/e2e-20260525-210000.log
══════════════════════════════════════════════
\`\`\`

## Log File Detail

For every test, the log file captures:
- Full request (method, URL, headers, body)
- Full response (status, headers, body)
- Expected vs actual values for assertions
- Timestamps for timing analysis

## Prerequisites
- \`curl\` (should be available everywhere)
- \`jq\` (for JSON parsing and validation)
- Deployed worker with valid csvkey

## Acceptance
- Script runs all 37 tests against a deployed worker
- All tests pass on a correctly-deployed worker
- Failed tests produce clear, actionable output (expected vs actual)
- Log file captures full request/response for debugging
- Exit code = number of failures (0 = all pass)
- --section flag allows running individual sections during development
- --verbose flag shows response bodies inline

title: Run cargo test + cargo clippy — full unit test suite and lint gate before E2E
description:
# Task: Full Cargo Test + Clippy Gate

## What
Run the complete unit test suite (all #[cfg(test)] modules across all source files) and cargo clippy against the WASM target. This is the final quality gate before deploying and running E2E tests.

## Why Separate from Phase 2 Clippy (2.10)?
Bead 2.10 runs clippy after Phase 2 modules are written (catches issues early in the business logic layer). THIS bead runs after Phase 3 is complete — it covers the orchestration code in lib.rs, the UUID generation, the routing, the metadata extraction, and the structured logging. It's the comprehensive final check.

## Commands

\`\`\`bash
# Run ALL unit tests (native target, tests all pure logic)
cargo test 2>&1 | tee tests/cargo-test-output.log
echo "Exit code: $?"

# Run clippy against WASM target (catches wasm-specific issues)
cargo clippy --target wasm32-unknown-unknown -- -D warnings 2>&1 | tee tests/clippy-output.log
echo "Exit code: $?"

# Verify formatting
cargo fmt --check

# Build for release (verify binary size)
worker-build --release
ls -la build/worker/*.wasm | awk '{print "Binary size:", $5, "bytes"}'
\`\`\`

## Expected Output

\`\`\`
running 96 tests  (14 errors + 15 providers + 39 validation + 20 dataforseo + 8 lib)
test errors::tests::truncate_under_limit_returns_full_string ... ok
test errors::tests::truncate_at_exact_limit_returns_full_string ... ok
...
test result: ok. 96 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out

    Checking rusty-llm v0.1.0
    Finished `dev` [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in X.XXs
    (no clippy warnings)

Binary size: ~350000 bytes (< 500KB target)
\`\`\`

## What to Do If Tests Fail
1. Read the test failure output carefully
2. Identify whether the test is wrong or the code is wrong
3. Fix the root cause (not the symptom)
4. Re-run cargo test until all pass
5. Do NOT suppress failing tests with #[ignore] unless documenting why

## What to Do If Clippy Warns
1. Read the suggestion
2. Apply the fix (clippy usually knows better)
3. If genuinely a false positive: #[allow(clippy::specific_lint)] with a // comment explaining why
4. Never blanket #[allow(clippy::all)]

## Acceptance
- cargo test exits 0 (all ~96 unit tests pass)
- cargo clippy exits 0 (no warnings with -D warnings)
- cargo fmt --check exits 0
- Release binary < 500KB
- Test output and clippy output saved to tests/ directory for audit trail
